speaker: interviewee
text: The biggest.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the interview [INTERPOSING VOICES] with Rose Minsky. I'm Leslie Troper, the interviewer. We're  in Brooklyn, New York, December 7th, 1994. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] Now you're living in East New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was living in East New York. About eight years, I was living over there. Then from there, we moved. Because that apartment was so cold that we couldn't stand no more. I rather accepted a project, to, to have the heat. And I was living in a project on the East, Lower East Side, on Jefferson Street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and my husband got the-- easy to go to work. He was working for the Jewish, for the Jewish So he got there-- that was in a distance of five minutes' walk. From, from this place, I moved here. Here, I'm already 30 years in [INAUDIBLE], houses on Neptune Avenue. We lived 30 years here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you raised your children here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I raised my children here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they got-- my son finished Brooklyn College, he went to California. He's many years over there. His home  is already there, not here. And my daughter married, also, a man that-- from Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name? Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rebecca. So she lives-- she was living for a number  of years in Canada. And then they both got jobs in Albany. So they moved to, to Saratoga. They travel to work to Albany. The children finished school, went  to college in, in Montreal. Both kids were born in America, my grandchildren. But then they moved there. And now, one is studying for-- to be an interviewer, like you do, in, in Vancouver. And one is in McGill University, Montreal. So the family got split up. I have family, thank God, but I don't have them nearby. Takes, takes years till I see my, my son, till he comes here. I've been there a couple times. You know, I used to travel much more than now. Now I don't travel no more so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What have you done, in recent years, to help deal with your experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing that I did was to be able to transmit, to let people know-- let young-- young children know what went-- what went on, in order it shouldn't be forgotten. So I think it's very important that I went to schools, I went to centers, and I talked to people. And I wrote a lot about my experiences. And now, they can be found, some of them, in the Washington museum, some in this museum. The first-- at the beginning, I gave it into the Holocaust Center on Avenue J, in Brooklyn. I used to go there to have lectures. They brought classes over there. Now they merged with the museum in New York that they're going to build. And all my files are here. My files. I feel that I have to do something for the six million and for my immediate family that I lost. That at least, from time to time, their names are being reminded. That they didn't perish in vain, that shouldn't be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] What reaction do you have from people, when you speak to them about what has happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean about reactions?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do people respond to you? You tell them your stories, your experiences. What is their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their reaction, they-- some of them, they can't believe that things like this could happen in the, in the 20th century. It's hard to believe. And they're getting very, sometimes, emotional. Some of the students are asking me very important questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an example. What do they ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For an example, one student, an 18-year-old high school student, a black one, asked me a question. Mrs. Minsky, how you feel now about the Germans? Now, how I feel? What is my attitude towards them? I said, I cannot feel very bad now. Because it's the third generation after Hitler. And I cannot feel very bad against the young kids. Unless I would know that they be taught anti-Semitism, that they're still Nazis-- I would hate them very much. He says, you know, Mrs. Minsky, I-- I cannot understand. The black one said that. He looked very tough. I would be afraid of him in the street. [LAUGHS] He said to me, you  know, Mrs. Minsky, I can't understand you. If I would be your life, if I would go through and lost the whole family, I would go to Germany, kill everyone who I see in the street. That's what he answered me, a tough guy. This, this further reaction it makes on them. It makes them-- some of them say, my grandma-- Jewish kids, for example-- my grandma went through also the Holocaust. But she don't want to talk about it. She don't-- I don't know nothing. She never wants to take up the subject. I admire you for this, that you-- and I thank you, very much. As a matter of fact, I got those letters that I show you. I got a lot of letters of students, that they expressing their appreciation that I told them about those things, that they didn't know that it was that bad. And I think it makes on them a, a very deep impression. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effects, if any, do you think your experiences had on the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to tell them when they were very young. But like, for example, my oldest son was asking questions. What happened to my-- why don't I have a grandmother? Why can't we go for holidays, like other kids go for holidays, to grandma? I had to tell them, and he grew up with this. I think it left on him a scar. Some scars it left on them. Some scars, that they are very emotional. My children are very emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you dream about what went on in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, in the beginning, yes, a lot of bad dreams, nightmares, a lot of nightmares. But I found out that more I talk about it, more I go to classes, to centers, and I talk about it, it calms me down, less I dream about it. Because I'm giving it out. I don't keep it in me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's 50 years since--

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 Years since it happened, yeah. 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you  like--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still see them like it would be yesterday. Still, I have them in my memory. I still see, see them everyday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say to future generations, as you, as you do so beautifully, you go to schools and you speak to students? What message do you want to leave for your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: For my grandchildren, there should be-- they should watch. They shouldn't let ignoramuses and fascists like Hitler to come to power. They, they should, they should start from the beginning. Like in the beginning, people used to say, oh, it's only a small group, they don't mean nothing. It means a lot. It shouldn't develop to a big thing. Like we see them on television. They bringing them up-- out on television, those Neo-Nazis, those groups, the talk shows. And I'm very angry for this reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do they give them publicity? That's what they want. For them-- because they want to make the money from it, they, they-- millions of people hearing their stupid statements, that the Holocaust never existed, that it never was there? That they want to kill all the Jews, still now, after 50 years. Why they let this happen? This angers me a lot. Because I thought I'm coming to a free country, and I'm rid  of it for good. But I still see it on television a lot. Do you do anything about that when you see it? Do you write letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote, yeah. I wrote a letter. Then it was once-- I even got  it. When it was with that Vanessa-- what's   her name, the actress? Vanessa Redgrave. And she played in a movie. was the movie. And I-- it was, the whole thing was not real. It was that the Nazis are music-lover and makes the people play beautiful music. He treats them better. And they made a real funeral for one of the musicians. It's not true. It never happened. No matter how talented you were, no matter how-- they treat you just like, like, like garbage in the camp. It's not true. They showed-- she showed the better face of them. It's  not true.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in response to that movie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote a letter to the, to the network. I wrote a letter that it's not true. That why they doing this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they answer you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have a letter, an answer, from the Northwestern University answered me. In the beginning, they said, they cannot control political views-- they call it political views-- of their professors. They cannot control them. But later, I got another letter from a very important man in the Holocaust. It was [INAUDIBLE] is his name. [INAUDIBLE]. I got another letter that-- saying that I should be happy to know that he, he was fired. He went back to Germany, that Professor [INAUDIBLE]. That no student want to attend. After letters start arriving like mine-- many people wrote, I'm not the only one who wrote. And they got many letters, and no student want to attend his classes. He had nobody to teach, so he left-- he left, went back to Germany. He was a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have some pictures, some documents, things you'd like to show us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you bring them over?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the big ones. This is my sister, two years younger than me. And she was shot in 1942, by a Nazi, trying to escape from a roundup, to be sent away to Treblinka. She was trying to run in a building to be hidden somewheres, in a attic or basement. But the Nazis shoot down her with machine-- from a machine gun, from a rooftop of the fifth floor, and killed her, instantly. This is a picture of my mother when she was a young girl. This is a picture of my grandmother, my mother's mother. And by the way, I want to mention that those older pictures I got from an uncle who lived here, in this country. And my mother used to send to him all the family pictures. And when I came here, the first thing I ask him is to show me his album. [LAUGHS] And I picked the pictures out. I made Xeroxes for myself. This is a picture of my father when he was a young man. This is the picture of my father and mother. And the little girl, that's me. And the little baby that my mother holds is my sister, who is not alive. This is a picture of my parents and my two young brothers vacationing, in the wintertime, in a resort place, [NON-ENGLISH], not far from, from Warsaw. This is a picture of the children. At that time, we were only three of us. The youngest still wasn't here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's me when I was-- I must been here about seven years old. And that's my sister, who was five years old, and my older brother, who was here a baby, like one and a half years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is you-- your daughter  or your  granddaughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Granddaughter, OK. So we have two pictures of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me in 1938, one year before the war broke out. With my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you just shut that light? He did. No, he did. Do you want me to turn it back on? Yeah.

words.text: me to turn it back on?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my son, Sam, when he-- we just arrived, shortly after we arrived to this country-- in a carriage. And as you can see, in the picture, I am expecting my-- another, another child, who was born already here in America. Oh, no, I have this  on me. I cannot do  it. Here, I'm lighting the candles on a Memorial Day, after the six million Jews. We have here a club that, each year, we commemorating the, the Holocaust. This picture is a long time ago, maybe about 12 or 15 [LAUGHS] years ago, in California, when I was visiting my son, Sam, with my youngest son, Jerry. This is my graduation picture from Touro College in 1981. These are my two granddaughters. One is studying in, in McGill University and working part-time in, in the office there. And one is studying-- is working already as a reporter in Vancouver on television. That's me, on the boardwalk, [LAUGHS] in Brighton Beach, with my son, Sam, when he was visiting a couple years ago, here, from-- from Los Angeles. And the-- about the television on that thing, on that too-- This picture, I am lecturing about the Holocaust, in the Center for Holocaust Studies, on Avenue A, before they merged with the Holocaust Museum in Manhattan. I was talking to the satellite high school students. And they were asking me many interesting question. I gave them answers. In this picture, I am with my husband in Washington. We were singing that time, in the Workmen's Circle Chorus. And we were singing Holocaust-- it was a Holocaust program in Washington, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with all the cabinet members on the stage. This was on the [NON-ENGLISH]. Here, we in the Workmen's Circle, commemorating the Holocaust. And I was lighting the first candle there. Not the [INAUDIBLE]. I don't need that.
